title: The Borrowed Heart
content: My heart has been trying to kill me since I was thirty-one. In 1996, I received a heart transplant. I was sixty-seven—old for a transplant. They told me I might get a few more years. That was almost thirty years ago. Someone's family donated their loved one's heart. I'll never meet them. I'll never be able to thank them properly. But I think about them every single day.
extended_content: Living on borrowed time changes you. Every day feels urgent. Every conversation matters. I don't have patience for small talk or pretense. I need to say what I came to say. I keep a picture of what I imagine the donor family looks like on my desk. I talk to them sometimes. Thank them. Tell them what I did with the extra years they gave me. Some people find that strange. I don't care. Gratitude doesn't need to make sense to anyone else. When people ask how I've stayed so committed to the same message for fifty years, I tell them: when you're living on time that isn't yours, you don't waste it on things that don't matter.
when_to_tell: when someone is facing mortality, health challenges, or needs perspective
lesson: Borrowed time is still time. Use it well.
